title: Treating Dark Circles With Dermal Filler. Causes, Safety & Result Expectations.
filename: Treating Dark Circles With Dermal Filler. Causes, Safety & Result Expectations..en.vtt
excerpt: How do you reduce dark circles this is one of the most common reasons that patients present particularly in their 40s and 50s and is to do with that shadow underneath the eye unfortunately this procedure is full of pitfalls and potential complications so let's have a look at how …

segments.text: how do you reduce dark circles this is one of the most common reasons that patients present particularly in their 40s and 50s and is to do with that shadow underneath the eye unfortunately this procedure is full of pitfalls and potential complications so let's have a look at how to reduce dark circles so last week I treated a patient and she presented with a sense of tiredness when she looked at herself in the mirror I identified that the shadow underneath her eye was one of the key reasons why she may appear tired in certain circumstances so the next challenge for me as a clinician is to decide whether the type of shadow that she's got is actually improvable with a dermal filler treatment or whether I need to recommend something else before we dive in don't forget to like And subscribe so that you never miss an episode it's important to note that there are actually two types of tear trough I believe there's a primary and a secondary type of shadowing so primary tear troughs children have you'll often see children with a shadow underneath their eye in fact my children have slight tear trough Shadows now these Shadows can trigger some patients to present often in their early 20s and early 30s when they've actually always had the problem the a

tricky bit for clinicians is that these are the hardest teardrops to treat so it's always important to think about how long the patient has had the Shadow and whether this is to do with a really strongly attached tear trough ligament or whether it is to do with aging which is the secondary tear trough problem so secondary tear troughs occur as the face ages you develop a shadow and there are actually multiple different reasons why this can happen but they are different to the primary tear trough when treating secondary tier troughs we usually have a bit more power to get a good result because we're correcting an imbalance in the fat pads that causes a shadow and because this imbalance in the fat pads is to do with aging we can often correct where the volume has decreased to hide or blend in some of that shadow but we can't do this quite as easily in a young person with a primary tear trough Shadow the ideal patient to treat is someone who has a secondary onset tear trough that means they never used to have it and now it's developed and you may also be able to tell by examining the patients whether it feels quite tight in the area so if it if it's quite mobile you can also test with a cotton Bud

pushing underneath the ligament if it elevates outwards that gives you another clue that you may be able to reject the skin outwards which means it'll catch the Light differently which means you'll reduce the shadow so you can do things on examination and on history that give you a bit more certainty about which type of tear trough it is so why are so many clinicians and patients a little bit afraid of doing the tear trough area now there is good reason for this it's because it really is the area most likely to need to be reversed so a lot of clinicians will say it's not worth the risk and this might be true in some hands but not true in others and true in some patients but not true in others so we have to figure out what are the variables that are making it not worth the risk and how can we mitigate some of those risks the first thing is the most common side effect from tear trough is Simply Having visible filler so you have a treatment the filler May improve the shadow but you get some other Shadow or a bulge or a shape or maybe some translucency or Tindle effect that essentially becomes another distraction which means is not worth having the product and we end up having to reverse

the whole procedure this happens in a cohort of relatively new injectors of maybe less than two years experience probably about five percent of the time that's certainly what I found when I audited a group of clinicians this is not too high but it's probably the highest rate of reversals of any procedure that we do and it's because there really is no room for mistakes very thin skin right where your line of sight is the most visible part of your face with only 0.2 millimeters of dermis to hide any imperfections so it does take a lot of practice and careful injecting to be able to consistently get good results but most importantly also takes the right product you can't use just any product in this area and it takes the ability to select the right patients you should actually be saying no to a lot of patients who ask for treatment in this area or may have a shadow underneath the area because they need something else often it's surgery let's break down the potential risks and think about how to reduce the risk of each one now a lot of people worry about blind this in this area it's actually not a particularly high risk area for blindness because

most of the vessels are not in this area if you think about the vessels most likely to be associated with blindness it's non-surgical rhinoplasty glabella treatments it's the super trochlear artery and we are actually not going that close to the super trochlear artery I know to the untrained eye we are getting quite close medially but it's probably about a centimeter away in most patients which in my mind is quite a big difference between high risk and low risk product matters a lot because if you use a standard product what you get is an attraction of fluid afterwards now when I first started in Aesthetics I didn't have access to any of the newer products I did start doing tear troughs under after being trained and the rate of reversal was higher and I was very afraid of doing tear drops I used to do very tiny Corrections basically I'd almost try not to do tear trough put tiny amounts in and then still sometimes you get puffiness that happened later but it did make me very good at doing tear troughs cautiously because I was so afraid of failure that I was in a different Zone compared with many of the other parts of the face but choose the right product and things get easier after choosing the right product assuming that you've also done the work

necessary in other parts of the face the tear trough is affected by volume in the cheek both laterally and medially and often you can improve the tear trough and make it much easier to get the final Improvement by doing the surrounding areas first this is probably a topic for another video but please think holistically and don't just inject the medial third of the tear trough on its own we've got to think about the cheek the tear trough on both the lateral aspect so the lateral orbital Junction and also the medial aspect and not just fill that medial third like many trainers seem to do for me that creates a very unhealthy look with a lateral logic Junction is depleted and the medial aspect is more likely to look over treated so be holistic and you'll get better results when it comes to actually treating the tear trough we need to be thinking about how to reduce that risk of Edema so we've used some questions to rule out patients at risk so the primary tear trough is one patient you wouldn't treat but also ask about periorbital edema particular in the mornings if the patient is very puffy in the mornings this can be a clue that after the procedure they're going to be even more puffy and this is why we

want to be using low doses no matter what we're doing or which patient it is we try and keep the dose as low as possible and the second line of defense is to I believe to inject more deeply so wherever possible I'm trying to inject on the periosteum I've always felt that with a cannula you gravitate more towards the middle level because the cannula bounces off anything that is resistant so connective tissue in the dermis or the periosteum you end up injecting usually in an intermediate depth which is much more superficial and much more likely to show up as lumpy or bumpiness or puffiness after the procedure so I'm always trying to put low doses on the periosteum and I do prefer using a needle although I also use a cannula in certain people so the first thing to do when you start injecting is make sure that you are where you think you are unfortunately many clinicians do get a little bit confused in the beginning stages and they think they're about to inject onto the bone in the shadow where the lateral logic Junction is but actually it's the shadow where the lower lid meets the cheek and that's actually just above the orbit and you can go straight into the

orbit through the orbital membrane if you haven't checked this now it does sound if you were if you're well trained or very experienced this won't make sense to you because you've been doing it correctly for so long but I've seen many clinicians in those early stages learning start to point their needle towards the wrong Shadow and it's very risky thing so we always start by checking there's periosteum underneath your needle you can do this with a cotton bud or you can do it with your finger and you can of course do it with a needle before you start injecting so you want to make sure that you're on a flat surface that's the periosteum and not pointing towards the orbit you can see in this clip that I'm using a BD needle I think there's some benefit to using a BD needle for new injectors I actually typically don't but I found it useful when training and when people are starting out with tear trough is to use a Quantified amount so you've decided how much you're injecting before you inject and it's impossible to inject more than that and this is helpful because one of the most common reasons why tear troughs are disappointing in the early stages is when a clinician has used for example a cannula been a little

bit heavy fingered heavy thumbed and ended up injecting too much and then you end up with way too much product an area where you can see it so the advantage of using a BD syringes I can quantify the exact amount I need and only inject that much so in this clip I'm using Juvederm volbella this product I've been using for many years remember if you're in the US like it's only recently been licensed but we have been using this product since about 2013 2014 so I have many years of experience with it I absolutely am sure that it causes less periopal edema than the other similar products like juvenile Ultra 2 and I am not a rep for Allegan I don't represent them at all this is just my experience I've also had a very similar experience with red density 2 if you would prefer using that product now what I like about these products is they seem to attract less moisture and they're very malleable so you can make an adjustment after you've injected them and they seem to stay as they are when you first put them in unlike many of the products that absorb moisture I'm using a 0.05 mil bolus with

each injection and aside from aspirating knowing my depth knowing that there aren't arteries in this area the rising of the tissue in this area is actually also a very reassuring thing that you are safe because you can see exactly where that product is going it's not going into a vessel and it makes me feel safe in terms of doing little boluses in this area make sure you like And subscribe if you found that helpful so that you don't miss the next show foreign [Music]
